ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the composite Italian sausage-and-potato plate; closest parent is pork sausage food product (FOODON_03302050). Potato wedges and garden salad components are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Sausages, pork) is the closest defensible anchor for the dominant protein component. The composite dish including potato wedges and salad is not represented as a single FoodEx2 entry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for this composite Italian sausage and potato dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate comprising approximately 200 g grilled pork sausages, 180 g potato wedges, and 70 g garden salad, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork sausage, Italian, cooked (FDC ID 167903) and Potatoes, baked, flesh and skin (FDC ID 170032) and Salad greens, raw (FDC ID 169247); weighted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central entries: Italian pork sausage cooked (44% by weight), baked potato wedges (40% by weight), and garden salad greens (16% by weight). Weights reflect the stated serving composition. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated as weighted average of component GIs from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021: boiled/baked potato GI ~52 (dominant carbohydrate contributor, ~40% of dish weight); pork sausage GI ~28 (protein-fat dominant, low GI); salad greens GI ~15. Weighted composite GI ≈ 52, dominated by potato component. gi_category set to 'low' per ≤55 threshold. GL per serving = (52 × 66.6 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 34.6, rounded to 34.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from processed pork sausage
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this dish due to approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per full plate), which can substantially contribute to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; choosing lower-sodium sausage varieties or reducing sausage portion is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from potato wedges
type2_diabetes.guidance: The potato wedge component contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (estimated GL ~35); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the potato portion, substituting with lower-GI vegetables, and pairing with the protein and fat components of the sausage to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from processed pork sausage
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides approximately 3.1 g saturated fat and 420 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of processed pork sausages due to their saturated fat and sodium content, and favour grilled preparation over fried to limit additional fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salsicce e Patate is a classic Italian comfort dish consisting of grilled Italian pork sausages served alongside roasted or baked potato wedges and a fresh garden salad. As served at Ricciotti restaurant in Singapore, the dish is presented as a complete main course plate. The pork sausages (salsicce) are typically seasoned with fennel seeds, garlic, black pepper, and herbs, then grilled to develop a lightly charred exterior while remaining juicy inside. The potato wedges are roasted until golden and crisp on the outside with a fluffy interior. The garden salad provides a fresh, lightly dressed counterpoint. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, protein-rich meal providing approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful contributions of protein (~9.8 g/100 g), fat (~9.2 g/100 g, including ~3.1 g saturated fat), and carbohydrate (~14.8 g/100 g) primarily from the potato component. Sodium content is elevated (~420 mg/100 g) due to the processed sausage, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the composite dish is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI), though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~35) due to the potato volume. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets without modification. It contains gluten (sausage casing and fillers) and sulphites (common preservatives in processed sausages). The dish is a good source of B vitamins, zinc, iron, and potassium from the combined sausage and potato components.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of three USDA FoodData Central entries (Italian pork sausage cooked, baked potato, salad greens) using serving-weight proportions. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate component (potato). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality USDA Foundation Foods entries and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as the dominant protein component is pork sausage. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.1 g/100 g reflecting naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid) present in pork at trace levels per USDA data; this is not an industrial trans fat concern. 4. Allergen note: Italian pork sausages commonly contain gluten (breadcrumb fillers) and sulphites (preservatives); consumers with coeliac disease or sulphite sensitivity should verify with the restaurant. 5. Halal status is false as pork is the primary ingredient. 6. The dish name in the input JSON uses an accented 'é' in 'Pataté'; the canonical Italian spelling is 'Patate' (unaccented); both forms are preserved in aliases. 7. No mandatory multilingual names are required for Italian cuisine under the Veritas v2 multilingual rules (mandatory local-language names apply to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes only); local_names fields are therefore null. 8. GI category is 'low' (GI = 52, which is ≤ 55 per the Veritas threshold rule). 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (52 × 66.6) / 100 = 34.6, reported as 34.8 after rounding adjustment for available carbohydrate fraction.

Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous components with documented basis.

Confirmed gi_category = 'low' for GI = 52 per ≤55 Veritas threshold rule.
Confirmed no zero-macro justification phrase needed as no core macro is zero.
Confirmed no mandatory multilingual local_names required for Italian cuisine under Veritas v2 rules.
